Approach 1: Deere Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today's value using a required return.

For Deere, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $3.7b. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for the next few years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates those to build a ten year path, with projected free cash flow of $12.4b in 2030. All of these future cash flows are expressed in dollars and then discounted to reflect time and risk.

On this basis, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $673.38 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $567.69, this implies Deere trades at roughly a 15.7% discount to that intrinsic value, so on this measure the shares screen as undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Deere Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Deere, the P/E ratio is a commonly used yardstick because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that each share generates. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when they are pricing in weaker growth or higher risk.

Deere currently trades on a P/E of 31.85x. That sits above the Machinery industry average P/E of 27.73x and the peer group average of 24.86x. On simple comparisons, the stock is priced at a higher multiple of earnings than many industry peers.

Simply Wall St also provides a Fair Ratio of 39.86x for Deere. data.title: Berkshire Dividend Debate Under Greg Abel And What It Means For Investors
data.description:
• Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) is the focus of fresh speculation about introducing its first-ever cash dividend.
  • Discussion has centered on a possible dividend decision under Greg Abel's leadership, potentially as early as the end of 2026.
  • The idea marks a potential shift from Warren Buffett's long-standing preference for retaining and reinvesting all earnings.

Berkshire Hathaway sits at the center of US corporate life, with businesses that span insurance, energy, rail, manufacturing, and consumer brands. The group also holds a large portfolio of listed equities, which ties its fortunes to broad equity and credit markets. In that context, any change in how Berkshire uses its cash, including the possibility of a dividend, matters for investors who watch the company as a barometer of corporate capital allocation.

If Berkshire were to declare a dividend under Greg Abel, it would signal a different approach to returning cash alongside existing tools such as share repurchases. For you as a shareholder or potential investor, the key questions are how a dividend might affect capital deployment inside the conglomerate and what it could indicate about the stability of Berkshire's long term cash generation.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Berkshire Hathaway by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Berkshire Hathaway.

Is Berkshire Hathaway's dividend sustainable? Check out what every dividend investor needs to know in our dividend analysis.

Speculation about a first-time cash dividend at Berkshire Hathaway is really a conversation about how Greg Abel might balance cash retention with direct returns to shareholders. Berkshire sits on large cash reserves and owns cash-generative businesses in insurance, utilities, and rail, so any move to introduce a dividend would likely start with a modest yield and a conservative payout ratio that leaves room for buybacks and acquisitions. For you, the trade off is clear: dividend income today versus cash that stays inside the conglomerate to be reinvested. Given the share price has recently slipped below its 50 day simple moving average and some analysts see near term earnings pressure, a sustainable dividend would need to be set at a level that can withstand periods of softer earnings without forcing cuts. It would also signal that management is confident in the durability of Berkshire’s cash flows under Abel, after years in which Warren Buffett preferred not to pay a dividend at all.

Advertisement

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Forecast earnings are expected to decline by about 3.5% per year over the next 3 years, so a new dividend could face pressure if cash flows weaken.
  • ⚠️ Once a regular dividend is in place, reducing or suspending it can hurt investor confidence, which raises the bar for how conservatively it must be set.
  • 🎁 Trading at good value compared with peers and the wider industry may give management more flexibility in how it combines buybacks and any future dividend.
  • 🎁 Shares are flagged as trading 40.8% below an estimated fair value, so any clear capital return framework could help some investors better assess the risk reward trade off.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on what Abel and the board say about capital allocation at future meetings, especially around cash priorities between acquisitions, buybacks, and potential dividends. Pay attention to how earnings and free cash flow trends develop in Berkshire’s core insurance and energy units, because those divisions are central to any sustainable payout. It also helps to track whether valuation metrics and analyst sentiment stay aligned with the idea of returning more cash, or instead point to a preference for continued reinvestment inside the business.

Marine Electricals (India)'s Earnings Per Share Are Growing

Generally, companies experiencing growth in earnings per share (EPS) should see similar trends in share price. That makes EPS growth an attractive quality for any company. To the delight of shareholders, Marine Electricals (India) has achieved impressive annual EPS growth of 40%, compound, over the last three years. That sort of growth rarely ever lasts long, but it is well worth paying attention to when it happens.

Top-line growth is a great indicator that growth is sustainable, and combined with a high earnings before interest and taxation (EBIT) margin, it's a great way for a company to maintain a competitive advantage in the market. EBIT margins for Marine Electricals (India) remained fairly unchanged over the last year, however the company should be pleased to report its revenue growth for the period of 13% to ₹8.5b. That's a real positive.

Are Marine Electricals (India) Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

It's a necessity that company leaders act in the best interest of shareholders and so insider investment always comes as a reassurance to the market. Shareholders will be pleased by the fact that insiders own Marine Electricals (India) shares worth a considerable sum. Holding ₹6.7b worth of stock in the company is no laughing matter and insiders will be committed in delivering the best outcomes for shareholders. That holding amounts to 21% of the stock on issue, thus making insiders influential owners of the business and aligned with the interests of shareholders.

It's good to see that insiders are invested in the company, but are remuneration levels reasonable? Well, based on the CEO pay, you'd argue that they are indeed. Our analysis has discovered that the median total compensation for the CEOs of companies like Marine Electricals (India) with market caps between ₹19b and ₹75b is about ₹26m.

Marine Electricals (India)'s CEO took home a total compensation package of ₹7.5m in the year prior to March 2025. That's clearly well below average, so at a glance that arrangement seems generous to shareholders and points to a modest remuneration culture. While the level of CEO compensation shouldn't be the biggest factor in how the company is viewed, modest remuneration is a positive, because it suggests that the board keeps shareholder interests in mind. It can also be a sign of a culture of integrity, in a broader sense.

Is Marine Electricals (India) Worth Keeping An Eye On?

Marine Electricals (India)'s earnings per share have been soaring, with growth rates sky high. An added bonus for those interested is that management hold a heap of stock and the CEO pay is quite reasonable, illustrating good cash management. The drastic earnings growth indicates the business is going from strength to strength. Hopefully a trend that continues well into the future. Marine Electricals (India) certainly ticks a few boxes, so we think it's probably well worth further consideration. Of course, profit growth is one thing but it's even better if Marine Electricals (India) is receiving high returns on equity, since that should imply it can keep growing without much need for capital.
